elements.sent-2411-001: As per semi-classical gravity, extremal black holes carry vanishing temperature.
elements.sent-2411-002: Then standard statistical mechanics entails that the ground state degeneracy of extremal black holes should be exponential of its entropy.
elements.sent-2411-003: This is a huge degeneracy and begs the question: what stops this degeneracy from getting lifted? For supersymmetric black holes, the answer would be supersymmetry.
elements.sent-2411-004: But for non-supersymmetric black holes, the answer is less than clear.
elements.sent-2411-005: This issue has been debated for quite sometime.
elements.sent-2411-006: e.g. Page has suggested there are no true degeneracies for non-supersymmetric extremal black hole \cite{Page:2000dk .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In fact the semi-classical analysis also indicates the presence of a gap and it has been suggested that Hawking's analysis itself may not be trustable below this gap \cite{Preskill:1991tb, Maldacena:1998uz .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Recently, for near-extremal Reissner-Nordstr\"{o m black holes, it has been shown that for low temperatures black hole entropy $S \sim \log{ T$, where $T$ is the temperature and the proportionality constant is positive \cite{Iliesiu:2020qvm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This relation implies a vanishing density of states near the ground state.
elements.sent-2411-010: These results are hard to reconcile with a sizeable ground state degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand, $S \sim \log{ T$ implies negative infinite entropy at zero temperature, which is hard to reconcile with a discreet spectrum, typical of any quantum system with finitely many degrees of freedom\footnote{The Schwarzian theory \cite{Kit1, Kit2, Maldacena:2016hyu, Maldacena:2016upp, Engelsoy:2016xyb, Stanford:2017thb, Mertens:2017mtv, Lam:2018pvp, Kitaev:2018wpr, Yang:2018gdb, Saad:2019lba, Iliesiu:2019xuh,Choi:2021nnq, Choi:2023syx , which this relation emanates from, has been reproduced in CFT descriptions \cite{Ghosh:2019rcj , but the CFT descriptions describe an effective string capturing an infinite number of black holes at one go, hence has infinitely many degrees of freedom.. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: There have been indications that $S \sim \log{ T$ behaviour is trustable only in a ``low but not too low" temperature regime \cite{Mondal:2023bcx .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Overall the true nature of the ground state and low lying spectra remains elusive.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this article, we decisively settle the issue by looking at a microscopic description of a non-supersymmetric extremal black hole.
elements.sent-2411-015: Most well known microscopic descriptions are two dimensional conformal field theories (CFTs) capturing the dynamics of an effective string wrapping a circle \cite{Strominger:1996sh, Maldacena:1997de .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Black holes, including non-supersymmetric ones appear as highly degenerate states in a CFT.
elements.sent-2411-017: Entropies of a great many non-supersymmetric black holes have been accounted for this way \cite{Breckenridge:1996sn, Maldacena:1996iz, Horowitz:1996ay, Sfetsos:1997xs, Maldacena:1997nx, Maldacena:1996ya, Horowitz:1996ac, Dabholkar:1997rk, Danielsson:2001xe, Cvetic:1996dt, Cvetic:1998vb, Cvetic:1998hg, Das:1997kt, Zhou:1996nz .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However this degeneracy is likely a consequence of conformal symmetry, rather than extremality.
elements.sent-2411-019: e.g. even non-extremal states have exponential degeneracies \cite{Horowitz:1996ay, Horowitz:1996ac,Danielsson:2001xe .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore the relevant CFTs are free which precludes any important effect interactions could have had\footnote{Wee thank Roberto Emparan for drawing our attention to this issue.. .}
elements.sent-2411-021: If one could consider the full fledged sigma model describing the effective string (e.g. \cite{Minasian:1999qn for the CFT in \cite{Maldacena:1997de ), perhaps the true origin of this degeneracy could be deciphered.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We consider a microscopic description where the low energy physics of the brane system is that of an effective particle, captured by a quantum mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-024: Interactions are captured in the potential.
elements.sent-2411-025: Unlike in a CFT, large degeneracy is atypical of quantum mechanics and hence such microscopic descriptions are perfect for settling this issue.
elements.sent-2411-026: Such a description was considered in \cite{Emparan:2006it , but the state counting amounted to counting brane intersections and hence was oblivious to the dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Instead we exploit the $D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D6$ black hole, for which the explicit microscopic Hamiltonian was constructed in \cite{Chowdhury:2014yca, Chowdhury:2015gbk .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our strategy would be to flip the orientation of one of the constituent D-brane stacks, say the $D6$ stack, so as to break supersymmetry completely \cite{Ortin:1996bz .}
elements.sent-2411-029: A closely related system was studied in \cite{Gimon:2007mh , which realized non-BPS extremal black holes as threshold bound states of BPS branes.}
elements.sent-2411-031: But no microscopic Hamiltonian was constructed in these works.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the present case, despite the lack of supersymmetry, we are able to determine the Hamiltonian to a significant extent, owing to supersymmetric nature of individual stacks.
elements.sent-2411-033: The resultant theory hosts 32 Goldstinos due to 32 broken supercharges of type $IIA$ string theory.
elements.sent-2411-034: The theory makes the absence of any ground degeneracy explicit.
elements.sent-2411-035: Also, the non-zero energy of the ground state implies that quantum mechanically there are no truly extremal non-supersymmetric states.
elements.sent-2411-036: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section \ref{d2-d2-d2-d6 we quickly review the supersymmetric $D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D6$ system.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In section \ref{snsusy We move on to analyze the $D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D2 \textendash \overline{D6}$ system.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In section \ref{sdisc we discuss implications of our results and future directions.}
elements.sent-2411-040: To begin with, we quickly gather the essentials of the $D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D2 \textendash D6$ system in type IIA string theory on $\mathbb{R ^{3,1 \times T^6$.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The three D2 branes wrap three disjoint two-cycles of the $T^6$, say $x^4 \textendash x^5, \, x^6 \textendash x^7$ and $x^8 \textendash x^9$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The D6 branes wrap the entire $T^6$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The branes intersect at a point, hence the low energy dynamics is that of an effective particle.
elements.sent-2411-044: Number of branes in various stacks amounts to charges carried by the black hole.
elements.sent-2411-045: The number of degrees of freedom of this effective particle grows quadratically with charges.
elements.sent-2411-046: For large charges the entropy of the corresponding black hole is $2\pi \sqrt{N_1 N_2 N_3 N_4}$, where $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$ respectively are the number of branes in four stacks \cite{Shih:2005qf .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In order to write down the worldline theory of the effective particle, another crucial piece of information is the amount of supersymmetry preserved by the system.
elements.sent-2411-048: The 10 dimensional type IIA string theory has the supercharges \begin{equation}Q = \overline{\epsilon}_L Q_L + \overline{\epsilon}_R Q_R\end{equation} where $Q_{L.R}$ are the left/right moving supercharges and $\epsilon_{L,R}$ are ten dimensional Majorana-Weyl spinors with opposite chirality.
elements.sent-2411-049:
Four stack of D-branes impose the following four conditions on $\epsilon_{L,R}$:  egin{equation}
n \epsilon_L = s_{45} \Gamma^0 \Gamma^4 \Gamma^5 \epsilon_R \, , 
n \epsilon_L = s_{67} \Gamma^0 \Gamma^6 \Gamma^7 \epsilon_R \, , 
n \epsilon_L = s_{89} \Gamma^0 \Gamma^8 \Gamma^9 \epsilon_R \, , \epsilon_L = s_{456789} \Gamma^0 \Gamma^4 \Gamma^5 \Gamma^6 \Gamma^7 \Gamma^8 \Gamma^9 \epsilon_R \, ,\end{equation} where $s$-s are signs and $\Gamma$-s are ten dimensional Gamma matrices.
elements.sent-2411-050: Simultaneous satisfaction of these conditions require \begin{equation}s_{45} s_{67} s_{89} = - s_{456789} \, , \label{smatch}\end{equation} in which cases, 4 supercharges are preserved.
elements.sent-2411-051: In remaining cases, no supercharges exist.
elements.sent-2411-052: Note that the individual signs are not very consequential for the worldline theory, only the relative signs are.
elements.sent-2411-053: To understand how the supersymmetry plays out at the level of the worldline Lagrangian, it is instructive to add stacks one by one.
elements.sent-2411-054: Since the theory is finally going to be reduced to 0+1 dimension, we do so to start with.
elements.sent-2411-056: The dynamics of zero-modes is same as the dynamics of D0 branes, captured by the BFSS model \cite{Banks:1996vh .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Alternatively one can think of the dynamics as zero mode dynamics of a stack of D3 branes, captured by $\mathcal{N =4$ super Yang Mills theory \cite{Witten:1995im .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: We find it more convenient to use the language of four dimensional supersymmetry, with the implicit assumption that the fields depend only on time.
elements.sent-2411-059: A $\mathcal{N =4$ multiplet consists of one $\mathcal{N =1$ vector multiplet $V=(A^\mu, \lambda)$ and three $\mathcal{N =1$ chiral multiplets $\Phi_i = (\phi_i, \psi_i), \, i=1,2,3$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Here we have expressed the on-shell field contents as (boson, fermion).
elements.sent-2411-061: The complex bosons $\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3$ respectively describe the complex coordinate/Wilson line of/along the $45, 67, 89$ two-cycles\footnote{e.g. for the D2 brane wrapping the 45 directions, $\phi_1$ will be the Wilson line along 45, whereas $\phi_2, \phi_3$ will be the transverse coordinates along $67, 89$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For the D6 branes all three scalars describe Wilson lines.. .
elements.sent-2411-063: The periodicity of the torus coordinates/Wilson lines will be ignored, as we assume the branes never fluctuate enough to see the compact nature of the two tori.
elements.sent-2411-064: In fact the six real scalars are related by $SO(6)$ rotations.
elements.sent-2411-065: The spatial components of the vector field $A^\mu$ describe the transverse coordinates of the branes along three flat directions.
elements.sent-2411-067: This is reflected in the rotation of four fermions, coming from four $\mathcal{N =1$ multiplets as well as in the $SO(6) \sim SU(4)$ rotation of scalars.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The overall $U(1)$ parts of the four fermions, coming from four $\mathcal{N =1$ multiplets, lead to 16 kappa symmetries signifying 16 broken supersymmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Note the $\mathcal{N =4$ super Yang Mills is oblivious to the orientation of the brane, which becomes evident in its coupling to the Ramond Ramond forms.}
elements.sent-2411-071: But this would not concern us, as only the relative orientation of different stacks matter for our purpose.
elements.sent-2411-072: For rest of the paper, we will take each stack to contain a single brane, rendering the overall gauge group to be $U(1) \times U(1) \times U(1) \times U(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus all fields will be numbers.
elements.sent-2411-074: Another stack of D-branes breaks half of the supersymmetry, preserved by the first brane.
elements.sent-2411-075: Thus a total 8 supercharges are preserved, amounting to $\mathcal{N =2$ supersymmetry in four dimensions.}

n \psi_3^{(1)} - \psi_3^{(2)}, \, \psi_1^{(2)} - \psi_1^{(3)}, \, \psi_2^{(3)} - \psi_2^{(1)} \, ,

elements.sent-2411-152:
We postulate the most general linear superpotential consistent with the Goldstones and Goldstinos  egin{equation}

elements.sent-2411-153: The potential has the form \begin{equation}V = V_{gauge} + V_D + V_F \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-154: Here $V_{gauge}$ comes from the spatial part of the gauge kinetic term and is a sum of pairwise terms $|\vec{X ^{(i) - \vec{X ^{(j) |^2 |Z^{(ij) |^2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: For generic values of $Z$ fields, this can be set to zero by taking $\vec{X ^{(i) - \vec{X ^{(j) = \vec{0 \, \forall i \neq j$, i.e. all branes to coincide along the flat directions.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Minimization of in F and D term potentials are not affected by this, since $\vec{X}$ fields do not appear there.
elements.sent-2411-157: The D-term potential $V_D$ is entirely fixed by the interaction of the gauge multiplets and hence is same as in the BPS case.
elements.sent-2411-158: The FI parameters were determined solely in terms of the background fields in the BPS case \cite{Chowdhury:2014yca, Chowdhury:2015gbk .}
elements.sent-2411-159: We expect this to continue in the present case.
elements.sent-2411-160: Finally we come to the F-term potential $V_F$, the detail of which are relegated to Appendix \ref{appF .}
elements.sent-2411-161: Most important feature of F-term equations is that they are no more holomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-162: This immediately requires a change of strategy in finding minima as compared to the supersymmetric case.
elements.sent-2411-163: In the supersymmetric case, the F-term equations are invariant under not only the $U(1) \times U(1) \times U(1)$ gauge symmetry, but an enhanced $\mathbb{C ^\star \times \mathbb{C ^\star \times \mathbb{C ^\star$ symmetry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The effect of D-term equations can be incorporated as gauging this enhanced symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-165: Thus it suffices to solve the F-term equations alone subject to the complexified gauge invariance.
elements.sent-2411-166: But now due to lack of holomorphicity there is no such enhanced symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-167: So one must write down the whole potential and find its minima numerically.
elements.sent-2411-168: It is not clear if the classical minima are degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-169: If not, quite clearly one has a unique ground state.
elements.sent-2411-170: Even if they are degenerate they may or may not have vanishing energy.
elements.sent-2411-171: If the global minima itself has a non-zero energy, that would imply that microscopically there are no extremal configurations even at classical level.
elements.sent-2411-172: Although we feel this is unlikely, our preliminary numerical search using Python did not find any zero energy minima.
elements.sent-2411-173: Most interesting possibility is that of large number of degenerate minima at zero energy.
elements.sent-2411-174: Now that there is no supersymmetry, quantum mechanically this degeneracy will be lifted leading to a unique ground state.
elements.sent-2411-175: Moreover, as shown in \cite{Mondal:2023bcx this would lead to exponentially many low lying states, separated from the rest of the spectrum by a gap.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Such spectra in particular leads to $S \sim \log{ T$ in a low but not too low temperature regime.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Besides presenting concrete evidence in the microscopic side that extremal non-supersymmetric black holes indeed have a unique ground state, our work also offers an explicit microscopic set up for studying statistical mechanics of black holes.
elements.sent-2411-179: The non-supersymmetric nature of the system entails that individual theories on various brane worldvolumes will undergo non-trivial renomalization group flow, which in turn will modify the quantum mechanics capturing the zero mode sector.
elements.sent-2411-180: Whereas it would be interesting to compute these modifications, we do not expect these to alter the essential physics.
elements.sent-2411-181: Coming to the elephant in the room, namely the extremal entropy, we note now that supersymmetry is gone, there are no protected quantities.
elements.sent-2411-182: Nevertheless given numerous instances of microstate counting accounting for non-supersymmetric entropy one would be hopeful that some notion of extremal entropy should persist in the microscopic description.
elements.sent-2411-183: Furthermore it should be fairly close to the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy for large black holes.
elements.sent-2411-184: However the sheer absence of any truly extremal states raises the question- how should one define the extremal entropy in the microscopic description? Should the potential minima have zero energy, then the logarithm of the number of minima would be a natural candidate for the extremal entropy at classical level, whereas at the quantum level the scenario proposed in \cite{Mondal:2023bcx will prevade.}
elements.sent-2411-185: On the other hand if a significant fraction of the classical minima fail to attain zero energy, the situation is far from clear.
elements.sent-2411-186: The angular momentum of the microstates is also of interest.
elements.sent-2411-187: For supersymmetric black holes this can be defined using Lefschetz SU(2) \cite{Denef:2002ru, Bena:2012hf and leads to the zero angular momentum conjecture \cite{Denef:2002ru, Sen:2009vz, Dabholkar:2010rm, Sen:2011ktd, Bringmann:2012zr, Chowdhury:2015gbk .}}
elements.sent-2411-188: But the extension of this notion to the present case is not straight forward, as the vacuum manifold is not a complex manifold.
elements.sent-2411-189: Lastly, two extremal black holes differing only in the signs of the charges have the same surface area hence same Bekenstein Hawking entropies.
elements.sent-2411-190: Although quantum entropy functions would be different, for large charges the difference should be subleading.
elements.sent-2411-191: This implies that the extremal non-supersymmetric entropy should not be too far from the that of the corresponding supersymmetric black hole.
elements.sent-2411-192: This may be useful in coming up with the definition of extremal non-supersymmetric entropy in the present microscopic description.
elements.sent-2411-193: This work was supported by Banaras Hindu University.
elements.sent-2411-194: It is a pleasure to thank all the participants of the Solvay workshop on near-extremal black holes at Brussels, 2024 for useful discussions.
elements.sent-2411-195: I am indebted to Anirban Basu, Anshuman Maharana, Ashoke Sen, Dileep Jatkar, Roberto Emparan and Simon Jones for valuable comments and discussions.. .

elements.sent-2411-197: Each $\phi$ is actually fixed by two equations.
elements.sent-2411-198: So we get a bunch of consistency conditions on $z$ fields, which could be treated as effective F-term equations.. \bibliographystyle{JHEP.}
